While reading [this](https://reproducingnetworkresearch.wordpress.com/2016/05/30/cs244-16-an-accurate-sampling-scheme-for-detecting-syn-flooding-attacks-and-portscans/) article, I noticed that the author talks about a memory leak in dpkt. It seems that they were trying to reproduce [this](http://mkorczynski.com/An%20Accurate%20Sampling%20Scheme%20for%20Detecting%20SYN.pdf) paper's results.

> Unfortunately, after writing most of our code and testing on smaller datasets, we ran it on a larger file and discovered a memory leak in the library. This made it tricky to run our code on very large pcap files, and ended up capping the max number of packets we could process  and analyze to 27 million (on a machine running with 32 GB of RAM). 

They go on to say the following in the feedback section of the paper:
> The main issue was with the memory leak in dpkt. We were able to get around it by just running our code on machines with a lot of memory (Felipe’s laptop has 16GB of RAM, and Keziah spun up an AWS instance with 32 GB of RAM and we stopped having problems). However, if you want to run locally on machines with less memory, I would avoid this library.

Specifically about this issue - I dont yet know where/what leak they found, but I am going to ping them about it. Hopefully we can reproduce & fix this, iff this is a problem with dpkt.

It is easier to blame this on python's internal memory allocator, which does not release memory back to the OS, but lets see. Maybe we can add some code patterns to the examples which could be used as a workaround (for not just dpkt, but for python performance/memory problems in general).

issue_title: keyring prints password to ps output on OSX
issue_body:
When calling the security command-line tool, keyring prints the password to ps output on OSX. keyring should use the interactive security prompt (security -i) and call.communicate('') instead of using add-<store>-password directly:

security -i
call.communicate('{} -a {} ... -U\n^D'.format(
    SecurityCommand('add', self.store), username, ...))

I recently had a situation where I was passing ```algorithm``` instead of ```algorithms``` to ```jwt.decode()```. Due to the ```**kwargs``` eating the ```algorithm``` parameter and the decode method looking at the encoded JWT to figure out what type of algorithm to use, everything appeared to be working the way you would expect, even though it wasn't actually doing what I wanted it to.

If there wasn't the ```**kwargs``` there, my code would have raised a TypeError instead and immediately let me know I wasn't using the API correctly. Personally, I think that is a very desirable. 

Would you be interested in a patch to remove ```**kwargs``` and replace them all with named arguments instead?

names = ['test']

issue_title: Hang with IPv6 resolution but no uplink
issue_body:
Hangups seems to stall when accounts.google.com resolves to an IPv6 address but no IPv6 uplink is available. The circumstances for this are probably rare, but Hangups simply stalls trying to connect to the IPv6 address. 

I propose that Hangups either has a fallback mechanism to IPv4 after a timeout period, or takes an argument which forces IPv4 / IPv6, in a similar manner to ping, dig, etc...

Package Control will be switching to use `oscrypto.trust_list` for TLS trust roots to solve:

 - https://github.com/wbond/package_control/issues/1002
 - https://github.com/wbond/package_control/issues/1079

In order to do this, we need to tweak `trust_list.get_list()`. Ideally we need:

 1. A way to filter `get_list()` by one or more [key usage strings](https://github.com/wbond/asn1crypto/blob/master/asn1crypto/x509.py#L395-L406)/[extended key usage OIDs](https://github.com/wbond/asn1crypto/blob/master/asn1crypto/x509.py#L1531-L1595) 
 2. A new method such as `get_list()` that will return the list os trust roots along with a `set()` of their key usage names and extended key usage OIDs

Thanks for writing this library (and blog post) just in time, it helped me get Let's Encrypt certs a few days ago :-)

Using it with DNSimple I had to intervene in the code, as it was creating wrong domains:
I'm controlling `mathdown.net` zone;
for `www.mathdown.net` it created `_acme-challenge.www.mathdown.net.mathdown.net`, 
for `mathdown.net` it created `_acme-challenge.mathdown.net.mathdown.net`.

The command executed by the hook was:
```
lexicon dnsimple create www.mathdown.net TXT --name _acme-challenge.www.mathdown.net. --content IquLz5DkyqvV1f37rpP4sO2IkLp6HQKDX0psSt_8aFI
```
Output, with printing and breakpoint in providers/dnsimple.py in `_request()`:
```
Namespace(action='create', auth_otp_token=None, auth_password=None, auth_token='[REDACTED]', auth_username='beni.cherniavsky@gmail.com', content='kDA2FinGwvNPkcn6QzbrGS-q1q6fzSmiQL2EkpZPRrc', domain='www.mathdown.net', identifier=None, name='_acme-challenge.www.mathdown.net.', priority=None, provider_name='dnsimple', ttl=None, type='TXT')

(Pdb) pp locals()
{'action': 'POST',
 'data': {'record': {'content': 'IquLz5DkyqvV1f37rpP4sO2IkLp6HQKDX0psSt_8aFI',
                     'name': '_acme-challenge.www.mathdown.net',
                     'record_type': 'TXT'}},

                     'Content-Type': 'application/json',

(Pdb) self.options.domain

```

and for mathdown.net:
```
+ lexicon dnsimple create mathdown.net TXT --name _acme-challenge.mathdown.net. --content NonxWWUM3KwgrW3rOTQyRnrzP-IHqEJRPedPpia1HbE
...
{'action': 'GET',
...
{'action': 'POST',
 'data': {'record': {'content': 'NonxWWUM3KwgrW3rOTQyRnrzP-IHqEJRPedPpia1HbE',
                     'name': '_acme-challenge.mathdown.net',
                     'record_type': 'TXT'}},
 'default_auth': None,
 'default_headers': {'Accept': 'application/json',
                     'Content-Type': 'application/json',
                     'X-DNSimple-Token': 'beni.cherniavsky@gmail.com:[REDACTED]'},
 'name': '_acme-challenge',
 'pprint': <module 'pprint' from '/usr/lib/python2.7/pprint.pyc'>,
 'query_params': {},
 'self': <lexicon.providers.dnsimple.Provider object at 0x7fb466b51310>,
 'url': '/domains/mathdown.net/records'}
(Pdb) p self.options.domain
'mathdown.net'
```

So what's going on?
data['record']['name'] is taken by DNSimple as-is, but its meaning is relative to the zone.
I needed to truncate it to `_acme-challenge.www` for `www.mathdown.net` and `_acme-challenge` for `mathdown.net`.

Why Cloudflare worked in your blog post?  It seems its API takes absolute name (https://api.cloudflare.com/#dns-records-for-a-zone-create-dns-record — note `--data '{"type":"A","name":"example.com","content":"127.0.0.1","ttl":120}'` in their example),
while DNSimple takes relative name (https://developer.dnsimple.com/v1/domains/records/#create — note `"name": ""` in their example).

=> It seems like providers/dnsimple.py is the one that should truncate names in most methods?
(And list.records should append zone back to names?)

issue_title: Lexicon providers invoke `print` directly, making it difficult for calling code to suppress or redirect output
issue_body:
Currently, the convention for providers is to invoke `print` directly. For code using Lexicon as a library, it would be helpful to use a pattern that allows for suppressing or redirecting output (e.g., to a log file).

Perhaps this is already possible, but I don't see a way to do so.

issue_title: Times of recurring events are not adjusted to DST
issue_body: If I today (16th of February) add a recurring event, say `* cooking @s wednesday 5pm @e 1h @r w`, then the time of this event is **5pm** for the **22nd of March**, but **4pm** for the **29th of March** (and onward). I was expecting an event set at a given time to happen at the time given _regardless of DST_. (Using etmtk 3.0.41)

dump() currently uses dumps(), which builds the entire output string before writing to a stream. Since dump() doesn't return a string, it can be done iteratively, which would help especially when the output stream is stdout (so you see output right away). Also see #21.

issue_title: Does not fix `E265 block comment should start with '# '` when comment starts with non-alphanumeric symbols
issue_body:
I wonder whether `autopep8` does not move non-alphanumeric symbols one space away from the `#` comment sign by design or this is a bug?

```python
autopep8.fix_code('#--')
u'#--\n'
autopep8.fix_code('#a--')
u'# a--\n'
autopep8.fix_code('#4--')
u'# 4--\n'
```

With exception for the `#`:

```python
autopep8.fix_code('##--')
u'# --\n'
```

More examples on not digits/letters:

```python
autopep8.fix_code('#%--')
u'#%--\n'
autopep8.fix_code('#@--')
u'#@--\n'
autopep8.fix_code('#^--')
u'#^--\n'
autopep8.fix_code('#&--')
u'#&--\n'
autopep8.fix_code('#>--')
u'#>--\n'
```

issue_title: Percent pixels passing for log analyzer gamma could be skewed
issue_body: As mentioned [here](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pylinac/8F-e8Db9dbA), the calculation of the pixels passing could potentially be skewed.

I have Paramiko and netmiko installed.  I created this simple script to connect to one of my routers.

from netmiko import ConnectHandler

issue_title: Autocomplete inherited methods when overriding in child class
issue_body:
From an [issue from Anaconda](https://github.com/DamnWidget/anaconda/issues/180):

When working with frameworks, a very common pattern is to inherit from a provided class and then overriding the class methods/fields to customize behavior.

When writing this new customized child class, a very helpful tool is the autocomplete-functionality of both the method that I'm trying to override and also the autocompletion of the required arguments. This functionality is provided in IDEs like PyCharm and PyDev.

# Example
```python
from django.db import models
    
class TestModel(models.Model):
    def sav
```

A dropdown should appear here with a suggestion to autocomplete to `save`, and writing a left parenthesis should then generate all the parameters:

```python
class TestModel(models.Model):
    def save(self, force_insert=False, force_update=False, using=None, update_fields=None):
        models.Model.save(self, force_insert=force_insert, force_update=force_update, using=using, update_fields=update_fields)
```

Maybe an autocompletion to `super(TestModel, self).save(...)` would be better, or not generating the super call at all would also be acceptable (though preferably it should be generated), but this is the behavior in the Eclipse plugin PyDev (a dropdown is generated in which you can choose the option: `save (Override method in models.Model)` when pressing the autocomplete keybinding `CTRL + Space`)

I.e. my suggestion is to make Jedi aware of a cursor being inside a (child) class that is inheriting from another (parent) class, and when trying to override methods *and fields* (defined in the parent class) from within the child class, Jedi should autocomplete the fields and methods and also their arguments.

issue_title: Provide a standardized way to run tests from cmdline
issue_body:
This idea originated from the discussion at https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/pull/987.
Right now the recommended way to run tests is to get a copy of the source distribution (namely via GIT) and run `make test` which will:

1) install psutil
2) install testing deps (unittest2, mock, pywin32 etc.)
3) run tests

It would be nice, though, to provide a way to quickly test the psutil installation via cmdline and have it documented. Right now, the undocumented way is:

```
python -c "from psutil.tests.runner import main; main()"
```

...but it's a bit twisted. CPython accomplishes this with (https://docs.python.org/devguide/runtests.html):

```
python -m test
```

It would be nice if psutil did the same:

```
python -m psutil.test
```

This should be implemented and documented in the official doc as a quick way to make sure whether psutil  works on the designated user platform (aka running tests) without cloning the GIT repo or downloading the tarball.

Currently the GIT cloned code / tarball allows you to `make test` and `make setup-dev-env` in order to install the testing deps (unittest2, mock, pywin32, etc.), which is another useful use case, so we should also be able to:

```
python -m psutil.test --install-deps
```

If `python -m psutil.test` fails for a missing dep, the error message should tell the user to use `--install-deps` first.

issue_title: NetNS assumption about /var/run/netns path could be relaxed
issue_body:
In some cases (Docker & friends) the network namespace is not bound to an entry in /var/run/netns

The NetNS constructor might allow for specifying an absolute path (e.g.: /proc/xxxx/ns/net) or a fd to that path. This will spare the need for creating a symlink.

This should be a fairly easy patch, even if I haven't yet tried doing that.
Please let me know if there is a workaround that I've not been able to find by myself.

@jazzmes and others: do we really need `netns exec …` analogue, or it is enough to have `NetNS` API?

(ref: https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/issues/84 )

issue_title: Flag to skip parsing on PE object instantiation

Relates to #124

issue_title: context manager should wait till it get's the lock.
issue_body:
Hello, 

The way current implementation of 'with-statement' is done, if it fails to acquire the lock, it throws an exception. So, we still need to wrap this with-statement with our own retry logic. I believe that the retry logic should be built into the context manager. We should not be required to write any retry logic in addition to this. What are your views ? 

```
# waste_time.py
# If you run multiple instances of this code, all the processes should wait for each other instead of throwing an exception.
import time
from redlock import RedLock
with RedLock("distributed_lock"):
  print("Critical Section started")
  for i in range(0,10):
    time.sleep(1)
    print("wasted %s seconds" % i)
  print("Critical Section finished")
 
```

@jrfonseca We have read in your README file that you don't have time to maintain this package and that you want to delegate the code to someone else. Is it still the case?

Currently `==` operation is checked based on the value of `start` and `end` MayaDT objects and returns `True` if `start` and `end` are same for the passed `MayaInterval` object. Code:

```
def __eq__(self, maya_interval):
    return (
          self.start == maya_interval.start and
          self.end == maya_interval.end
    )

```

However, I feel that we should make the interval check based on the duration in seconds. For example:
```
MayaInterval(today, tomorrow) ==  MayaInterval(yesterday, today)
```

should return `True` because the interval is same. I think this way it will be more _Humanized_ based on the name "interval". if someone want to do check on `start` and `end` as well, they can still perform explicit check based on `start` and `end` attributes of the `MayaInterval` object.

If it sounds good, I can raise a PR along with allowing the other comparison operations on the `MayaInterval` objects.

        print k,v

Is there a way to pragmatically distinguish these special teams type events from regular offensive plays and thus allocate the points to a defense vs an offense?

issue_title: Messages sent to both Vector virtual CAN channels when specified to be sent to only one channel
issue_body:
Hi, I'm testing on Windows platform using Vector's virtual CAN bus. Here's my hardware config:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17481387/39379192-89d5fbd4-4a28-11e8-8d34-7dd184cddb99.png)

I'm using the following code to try to send just one message to each channel:

```python
import can
import time

bus1 = can.interface.Bus(bustype='vector', channel=0, bitrate=500000, app_name='python-can')
bus2 = can.interface.Bus(bustype='vector', channel=1, bitrate=500000, app_name='python-can')

msg1 = can.Message(arbitration_id = 0xa1,
                   data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8],
                   extended_id = False)
msg2 = can.Message(arbitration_id = 0xa3,
                   data = [8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1],
                   extended_id = False)
bus1.send(msg1)
time.sleep(1.0)
bus2.send(msg2)

bus1.shutdown()
bus2.shutdown()
```

I use BUSMASTER to read messages on the virtual CAN buses. Here it looks like each time the same message was always sent on both channels:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17481387/39379353-f273f1dc-4a28-11e8-8d27-b2f735fdf01f.png)

I was expecting msg1 on bus1 and msg2 on bus2. Did I miss something here?

Originally reported by: **Brian Thorne (Bitbucket: [hardbyte](https://bitbucket.org/hardbyte), GitHub: [hardbyte](https://github.com/hardbyte))**

---

The interfaces in `can/broadcastmanager.py` should take python-can `Bus` instances not channels - this would be more backend agnostic.

It might be worth adding this periodic api directly to the bus itself too.

---

```
def __init__(self):
         self.dataclose = self.datas[0].close

[...]

def next(self):

issue_title: License name should be MIT/Expat instead of X11
issue_body:
Hi,
First of all: thank you for this incredibly useful library!

It seems to me that the license you use is actually the MIT/Expat License as provided by the Open Source Initiative (http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT).
It is slightly different from the MIT/X11, which contains the additional following condition:

"Except as contained in this notice, the name(s) of the above copyright holders shall not be used in advertising or otherwise to promote the sale, use or other dealings in this Software without prior written authorization."

Sources:
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MIT_License
- http://directory.fsf.org/wiki/License:X11

Please can you kindly update your license file accordingly, or how you call it in your comments?
Thank you for the minor correction and many thanks again for the library!

Hello!

You probably may have many things more important to do, but I'm putting this idea here if you want to give me your opinion.

But my thought is what do you think of adding an argument to exlude some images to not check ? 

Because when you use official images of mongo, elastic, or whatever, they often have automatic build process. The impact is that image ID can change and not the tag. So I have often critical reports to just pull and image with a same tag and recreate the exact same container.

issue_title: Progress bar display does not update
issue_body:
#### Description

I found with the latest version on PyPI, 3.35.0, that a progress bar's display does not get updated until the end when it is finished.  I tested with the 3.34.3 version from PyPI, and this version updates the progress bar's display as expected.

#### Code

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import time
import progressbar

bar = progressbar.ProgressBar()
for i in bar(range(100)):
    time.sleep(0.02)
```

#### Versions

- Python version:
```
3.6.4 |Anaconda, Inc.| (default, Jan 16 2018, 12:04:33) 
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Clang 4.0.1 (tags/RELEASE_401/final)]
```
- Python distribution/environment: Anaconda
- Operating System: macOS
- Package version: `3.35.0`

## Checklist

- [x] I have verified that I am using a GIS-enabled database, such as PostGIS or Spatialite.
- [x] I have verified that that issue exists against the `master` branch of django-cities.
- [x] I have searched for similar issues in both open and closed tickets and cannot find a duplicate.
- [x] I have reduced the issue to the simplest possible case.
- [ ] I have included a failing test as a pull request. (If you are unable to do so we can still accept the issue.)

## Steps to reproduce

python manage.py migrate cities 0001_initial

## Expected behavior

No errors

## Actual behavior

psycopg2.IntegrityError: column "continent_code" contains null values

Full output (including Traceback):
https://gist.github.com/leibowitz/0427c3e70ffb07d81e1ecf82b349cf00

This is due to the last `RemoveField` of the [0002_continent_models_and_foreign_keys.py](https://github.com/coderholic/django-cities/blob/422e92d9ceaa07444d8fd6de00a526c72bf8e9ca/cities/migrations/0002_continent_models_and_foreign_keys.py#L83-L86) migration

As the documentation mentions:

> The operation is reversible (apart from any data loss, which of course is irreversible) if the field is nullable or if it has a default value that can be used to populate the recreated column. If the field is not nullable and does not have a default value, the operation is irreversible.

Source: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/migration-operations/#removefield

Adding `null=True` to the `Country.continent` field definition https://github.com/coderholic/django-cities/blob/422e92d9ceaa07444d8fd6de00a526c72bf8e9ca/cities/migrations/0001_initial.py#L64 solves the issue.

I imagine retrospectively adding a migration between 0001 and 0002 (to add `null=True`) would have prevented this issue, but it is a bit too late to do so.

The only way, in my opinion, is to add the `null=True` to the original definition - which shouldn't impact anyone already using `django-cities` in it's latest state.

I can see that there is little benefits to include this change, but would you consider merging this if I sent this change as a PR?

This seems to be a point of confusion (for me, at least) in any plotting package. Perhaps you can shed some light on the situation in the case of IPyVolume.

```
from numpy import *
import ipyvolume as ipv
```

# Surface Plots :)
Say we create a mesh via
```
x = linspace(0,1,11)
y = linspace(0,1,11)
X, Y = meshgrid(x,y, indexing='ij')
Z = X - Y
```
I'm not sure why indexing='ij' isn't the default behavior for meshgrid, but it makes the most sense to me.
The dimensions are actually ordered logically. `X[:,j] == x` and `Y[i,:] == y` for any `i`, `j`.

If we then do
```
ipv.figure()
ipv.plot_mesh(X, Y, Z)
ipv.show()
```

Then the labeled dimensions match my expectation. The surface increases with x and decreases with y.

# Volume Plots :(

However, the same logic doesn't seem to carry over to volume plots.

Consider
```
x = linspace(0,1,11)
y = linspace(0,1,11)
z = linspace(0,1,11)
X, Y, Z = meshgrid(x,y,z, indexing='ij')
F = X - Y
```

As before, `X[:,j,k] == x`, `Y[i,:,k] == y`, and `Z[i,j,:] == z` for all `i`, `j`, `k`.

But doing
```
ipv.figure()
ipv.volshow(F)
ipv.show()
```
I expect:
- F increases in x
- F decreases in y
- F is constant in z

I observe:
- F is constant in x
- F decreases in y
- F increases in z

So x and z are flipped. The situation is remedied by transposing `F` before plotting. In that case, my observation matches my expectation.

# Analogy to matplotlib

It may not be a coincidence that in `matplotlib.pyplot.contour`, (Assuming X, Y, Z defined as in `plot_surface` example) if you try `contour(Z)`, the plot comes out reversed, and it's necessary to do `contour(Z.T)`, however doing `contour(X,Y,Z)` works as expected.

So in matplotlib, specifying `X` and `Y` makes everything consistent. It seems that it's not possible to specify `X,Y,Z` for `ipyvolume.volshow` in the same way it is for matplotlib. Or is it, and I'm missing something?

Any thoughts are appreciated in understanding this apparent inconsistency.

Thanks!
Oliver

issue_title: Rollback transaction issue running under freetds
issue_body:
I'm using freetds 0.95.18 with unixodbc 2.3.2_1 on Mac OS X 10.9.x to talk to a SQL Server 2008 R2 database.  When Django tries to save records to the database, it fails with this error (both with Django 1.7 and Django 1.8 and their corresponding django-pyodbc-azure packages):

django.db.utils.ProgrammingError: ('25000', '[25000] [FreeTDS][SQL Server]The ROLLBACK TRANSACTION request has no corresponding BEGIN TRANSACTION. (3903) (SQLEndTran)')

As far as I can tell, it appears to be coming from the rollback call in the _set_autocommit method in base.py when autocommit is being set to False but no SQL has been issued yet.  For Django 1.7, I was able to work around this issue by overriding _set_autocommit and checking the connection's is_dirty method to prevent a rollback if the transaction wasn't "dirty".  But I'm in the process of upgrading to Django 1.8 and it looks like is_dirty is no longer on the base connection class.  Not sure yet what a good fix would be.

When running under Windows and the Microsoft "ODBC Driver 11 for SQL Server" driver, I don't experience this problem.

I also tried it on a baseline startproject/startapp project and had a similar experience, including when running the "createsuperuser" management command.

Here's my DATABASES section for reference:

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'sql_server.pyodbc',
        'NAME': '*****',
        'USER': '*****',
        'PASSWORD': '*****',
        'HOST': '*****',
        'PORT': '1433',
        'OPTIONS': {
            'driver': 'FreeTDS',
            'host_is_server': True,
            'extra_params': 'TDS_VERSION=7.3',
        }
    }
}

What I report below is a minor issue, that surprised me (worked around now).

Running `flake8 --format=junit-xml --output-file=flake8.xml` twice results in an invalid `flake8.xml` file (second run's output appended to previous runs output):

```sh

<?xml version="1.0" ?>
<testsuites/>

<?xml version="1.0" ?>
<testsuites/>

issue_title: Introduce a DbServer and turn the command oq-engine into a client
issue_body:
This is preliminary to the remove postgres project. Once we have a client-server architecture in place, all interactions with the database and the datastore will be mediated by the server, so the issues of a multiple user situation will disappear. Only the `openquake` user will access the database. If the
Web UI is active, it will act as db server too; otherwise the supervisor will have to start/stop the dbserver
with the commands

```bash
$ python -m openquake.server.dbserver &
$ python -m openquake.server.stop
```

One section will have to be added to `openquake.cfg`, something like

```
[dbserver]
host = localhost
port = 1999
authkey = changeme
```

There's service `increase-nf_conntrack-connections.service` installed by Deis. On new CoreOS installation it fails because there's no conntrack module loaded by default in CoreOS 557. I have some questions/proposals regarding it. Here's what (as it seems to me) should be changed, please let me know if I misunderstand the purpose of this service.

1. Conntrack connections limit should persist after reboot. This can be done by either installing `increase-nf_conntrack-connections.service` or by adding following to `write_files:` section:

```
# /etc/sysctld.d/conntrack.conf
net.netfilter.nf_conntrack_max=262144
```

2. Same for module `nf_conntrack`. It should be either loaded in the service before calling `sysctl` or added as a file:

```
# /etc/modules-load.d/conntrack.conf
nf_conntrack
```

issue_title: Strange issue on deployment and/or scaling
issue_body:
Sometimes we deploy one of our APIs and find that two of three containers are operating normally, yet one is throwing HTTP 500s. We can scale that app down to 1, then back up to three and have have the same issues but on different nodes. Our general process for deploying is to scale the apps down to one, do a `git push`, then scale back up to the desired number. This seems to have the best results, but we still end up with the situation described above now and then.

Scaling down also isn't ideal as we're running Node.js and this means a single process under heavy load.

There are no meaningful log entries from the failing container either, any ideas where to look to try and resolve this?

issue_body:
I created a user when bringing up the cluster (which got admin) and have no idea what the user/pass was. I registered a second user (and maybe a third) which are non admins...
How can I manage users? I have deisctl access.

## Issue/Feature

'charm build' needs to generate hooks in the hooks/ directory for relations, copying the hooks.template into place.

### I expect/expected the following

'charm build' generates hooks for all my relations, as defined in metadata.yaml

### What I got

'charm build' only generates hooks for relations included from interface layers. It does not generate hooks for relations with implementations embedded directly in hooks/relations (such as peer relations, which serve no purpose in an external repository), or relations with no need for an interface (such as juju-info, or relations being handled by standard reactive handlers or legacy charm-helpers style code).

Workaround is to manually create the entries in the hooks/ directory, although this does not keep the hooks in sync with the defined hook.template

issue_title: Getting oauth_problem=signature_invalid from Flickr when trying to upload
issue_body:
Hello, I'm trying to upload an image to flickr and receiving a `signature_invalid` error.

This code should reproduce:

```python
def get_flickr_session():
    # returns flickr session
    flickr = OAuth1Service(
        name='flickr',
        consumer_key=FLICKR_API_KEY,
        consumer_secret=FLICKR_API_SECRET,
        request_token_url="https://www.flickr.com/services/oauth/request_token",
        access_token_url="https://www.flickr.com/services/oauth/access_token",
        authorize_url="https://www.flickr.com/services/oauth/authorize",
        base_url="https://api.flickr.com/services"
    )

    auth_params = {
        "perms": "delete"
    }

    request_token, request_token_secret = flickr.get_request_token(
                                            params={'oauth_callback': 'oob'})
    authorize_url = flickr.get_authorize_url(request_token, **auth_params)
    print 'Visit this URL in your browser: ' + authorize_url
    pin = raw_input('Enter PIN from browser: ')

    return flickr.get_auth_session(request_token,
                                   request_token_secret,
                                   method='POST',
                                   data={'oauth_verifier': pin})

def upload_to_flickr(session, url, title):
    post_url = """https://up.flickr.com/services/upload/"""
    
    photo_location = download_file(url)
    
    headers = {
        'Content-Type': 'application/xml'
    }
    
    data = {
        "title": title,
        "photo": open(photo_location, 'rb').read(),
        "is_public": 0
    }
    
    return session.post(post_url, data=data)

session = get_flickr_session()
p = upload_to_flickr(session, "http://i.imgur.com/K8v9RgV.jpg", "test photo")
```

The response I get is `401` and the response text says `oauth_problem=signature_invalid`.

I see this is related to the closed issue #109, if this should go in that closed thread I'll move it.

issue_title: Question - Pipfile usage: From dev to production
issue_body:
I've been thinking about the usage of `Pipfile`, and this question kept bugging me:
Is the `Pipfile` for a project meant to be the same file used both for the dev and prod environments (and any other environment, like QA setups and such, if it applies)?

If yes, how do you do so? 
In development, users will usually install one (or many) packages in editable mode, which doesn't match how it should be installed when in production.

issue_title: What is the proper way to specify min version of setuptools to handle the package.
issue_body:
With setuptools having releases so often it'd be nice to be able to "require" certain setuptools version in the package.

Is it currently possible via `install_requires` / `setup_requires`? Or some other option needs to be added?

issue_title: WebApp deployment crashing after WebApp stop
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
Sometimes during a zip deployment, az crashes with the following exception:

```
ERROR: Object of type 'frozenset' is not JSON serializable
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/site-packages/knack/cli.py", line 201, in invoke
    self.output.out(cmd_result, formatter=formatter, out_file=out_file)
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/site-packages/knack/output.py", line 134, in out
    output = formatter(obj)
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/site-packages/knack/output.py", line 40, in format_json
    separators=(',', ': ')) + '\n'
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/json/__init__.py", line 238, in dumps
    **kw).encode(obj)
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 201, in encode
    chunks = list(chunks)
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 430, in _iterencode
    yield from _iterencode_dict(o, _current_indent_level)
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 404, in _iterencode_dict
    yield from chunks
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 404, in _iterencode_dict
    yield from chunks
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 404, in _iterencode_dict
    yield from chunks
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 437, in _iterencode
    o = _default(o)
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/site-packages/knack/output.py", line 32, in default
    return json.JSONEncoder.default(self, o)
  File "/opt/az/lib/python3.6/json/encoder.py", line 180, in default
    o.__class__.__name__)
TypeError: Object of type 'frozenset' is not JSON serializable

```

My guess's because the application isn't fully stopped by `az webapp stop` command, so there's a DLL blocked by WebApp IIS.

> Edit:
> I switched `sleep` command for: `while [[ "$(az webapp show --name ${AZURE_APP_WEB} --resource-group ${AZURE_RG} --query 'state' --output tsv)" != "Stopped" ]]; do sleep 2; done` and the problem still happens. Probably it's not related to `webapp stop`.


**To Reproduce**
This behavior is inconstant, but it occurs in 1 out 10 tries in my environment.

1 - Stop webapp
`az webapp stop --name ${AZURE_APP_WEB} --resource-group ${AZURE_RG}`

2 - Try to upload a new version zip 
`az webapp deployment source config-zip --name ${AZURE_APP_WEB} --resource-group ${AZURE_RG} --src api.zip`

**Expected behavior**
I expected the webapp fully stop after `az webapp stop` command and deployment succeed.

**Environment summary**
Bitbucket pipelines using official microsoft/dotnet:2.1-sdk image as base. Az Cli is installed with:
```
RUN apt update && \
    apt install lsb-release apt-transport-https -y && \
	AZ_REPO=$(lsb_release -cs) && \
    echo "deb [arch=amd64] https://packages.microsoft.com/repos/azure-cli/ $AZ_REPO main" | \
    tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/azure-cli.list && \
	apt-key adv --keyserver packages.microsoft.com --recv-keys 52E16F86FEE04B979B07E28DB02C46DF417A0893 && \
	curl -L https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc | apt-key add - && \
	apt update && \
	apt install azure-cli
```

**Additional context**
I tried to add a `sleep 5` command after the stop, the number of errors considerably decreased.

### Environment summary

(Same as in my last issue.)

---

### Description

The documentation of `az storage blob upload-batch` is really unclear.

1. It seems to me that the description of the destination argument has a few occurrences of "source" that should really be "target".

2. The doc for the source argument is ungrammatical in a confusing way too.

3. It fails to specify what happens when the source directory has nested directories: is the structure copied? flattened? or maybe subdirs are ignored?

5. It is really unclear what "shape" should a URL destination have.  An example would help a lot.  (I had to read the source, and the only thing I could figure is that it can be the container name, which I used.)

6. It also fails to mention that the destination can be "<container-name>/<subdir>".

7. Slightly unrelated: it'd be nice if the syntax of this would be similar to the syntax of the `upload` subcommand.  Even better, if there was only `upload`, and it had a flag specifying that the source is a glob pattern for several files to upload.

issue_title: "sfctl application upgrade" seems to be broken
issue_body:
It appears that upgrading a service-fabric application via `sfctl application upgrade` is completely broken.

I'm attempting to upgrade using the stand procedure documented here: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/service-fabric/service-fabric-application-lifecycle-sfctl#upgrade-application

I can upload and provision the new version fine, and it shows up in the Service Fabric explorer.  But running `sfctl application upgrade --app-id sf-test --parameters "{}" --health-check-stable-duration 60 --upgrade-domain-timeout 1200 --upgrade-timeout 3000 --mode Monitored` throws a 500 error:

`Error occurred in request., RetryError: HTTPConnectionPool(host='localhost', port=19080): Max retries exceeded with url: /Applications/sf-test/$/Upgrade?timeout=60&api-version=6.0 (Caused by ResponseError('too many 500 error responses',))`

However, I **can** successfully upgrade the app with this PowerShell command: 

`Start-ServiceFabricApplicationUpgrade -ApplicationName "fabric:/sf-test" -ApplicationTypeVersion "2.0.0" -HealthCheckStableDurationSec 60 -UpgradeDomainTimeoutSec 1200 -UpgradeTimeout 3000 -FailureAction Rollback -Monitored`

I also tried this on the EchoService and VisualObject samples in this repo:  https://github.com/Azure-Samples/service-fabric-java-getting-started

Same deal.

My Environment:

Bash on Ubuntu 16.04 LTS, installed sfctl via: `sudo curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/service-fabric-scripts-and-templates/master/scripts/SetupServiceFabric/SetupServiceFabric.sh | sudo bash`
http://blogs.msdn.com/b/windowsazurestorage/archive/2015/04/29/getting-started-with-client-side-encryption-for-microsoft-azure-storage.aspx

issue_title: too much magic - what would it take to have an object oriented interface?
issue_body:
Hello sacred authors! Great work - I'm just starting to embrace sacred and am feeling very excited about the potential.  

So far I've been finding it just a bit too magical for my tastes though.  I think that the magic has it's place in reducing the boilerplate to almost zero for a quick experiment - but I've been finding it to be somewhat of an activation barrier in getting started for someone not familiar with the library. 
 
The issue is that sacred breaks completely out of the standard python programming paradigm, which makes it difficult to reason about code behavior.  

Again, I do think that this has it's place for quick scripting - but for production workflows that prioritize extensibility and maintainability over line count, I think an object-oriented interface would be more appropriate. 

My reasoning is that:

- the object hierarchy (which I feel pretty confused about right now) would be explicitly defined in the code, it's clear exactly an experiment is made up of
- easier to integrate with existing code. I know how classes behave, how inheritance works, how to work them into module structure. It's unclear to me how to effectively use the magic interface to scared within a larger project and not as a one-off thing. 
- much easier to get less engineer-minded lab members to used sacred when they don't have to learn a whole new paradigm. They just have to fill in a template

Any estimates how challenging this would be? Recommendations on where to start?

issue_title: noarch only repositories do not work as expected
issue_body:
A http based conda repository with only:
```
/channel-name
   /noarch
      repodata.json
      [packages]
```
Will not work for _any_ platform, as the sub-folder for a given os-bits (win-64/osx-64 etc) [must exist as a requirement](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/972ad5d5f6d4c987d683aa6bedfbed3ba7753c5c/conda/fetch.py#L107).  The workaround we use currently is to make empty folders for the supported platforms with empty ```repodata.json``` and ```.index.json```, but it feels hacky:
```
/channel-name
   /noarch
      repodata.json
      packages ...
   /win-64
      repodata.json
      [no packages]
```
In the related fetch code [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/972ad5d5f6d4c987d683aa6bedfbed3ba7753c5c/conda/fetch.py#L124) if the "noarch" folder missing it is ignored silently, can we do a similar thing for platform-bits?  I am happy to file a PR on this if that is an acceptable solution.  It add's the capacity for silent failures of repositories, which does not feel good.  Perhaps provide a list of failed repo-fetches at the end?  From this angle, I can see some clear reasons that noarch needs to be treated specially.  

Similarly I also think it seems sane to treat the noarch and platform folders similarly, as the ideal behavior is basically merging the noarch and platform package list at a later step.

See also:
https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/462

issue_title: pre/post script failures appear to be fatal but only produce WARNING
issue_body:
While looking into #5417 I have discovered that the `pre-unlink.sh` script's failure is actually causing the entire update to bork out, but I am not given any indication that this has happened.

Here is the fragment showing the `WARNING` followed by (effective) failure:

```
Proceed ([y]/n)? y

pysocks-1.6.6- 100% |#############################################################| Time: 0:00:00   1.73 MB/s
urllib3-1.21.1 100% |#############################################################| Time: 0:00:00   4.76 MB/s
widgetsnbexten 100% |#############################################################| Time: 0:00:00   3.63 MB/s
WARNING conda.core.link:run_script(519): pre-unlink script failed for package conda-forge::widgetsnbextension-2.0.0-py36_0
consider notifying the package maintainer
(root) twitter $
```

And also strangely it appears that either the unlink phase and package downloads are concurrent or unlinks are done one-at-a-time with each download, because on my first invocation of the update command 3 packages were downloaded before the pre-unlink script failure caused the process to abort, but when I immediately re-issued it more downloads occurred and the command completed successfully.  I would have expected all the downloads to complete before conda started running `pre-unlink.sh` scripts.  If that is a reasonable expectation then perhaps there is another issue that should also be reported.

Attached is the trace of executing exactly the same command (`conda update -n spark --all`) 3 times in succession:

[warning_trace.txt](https://github.com/conda/conda/files/1159906/warning_trace.txt)

First time: downloads 3 packages and then fails on `pre-unlink.sh` with a WARNING.  Unclear that the update failed.

Second time: many more packages downloaded, appears to complete successfully.

Third time: confirms that the second invocation worked: no new packages to install/update.

https://github.com/plaid/plaid-python/blob/master/setup.py#L20 uses an `==` constraint to pin `requests` to an exact version.  This prevents automatic security updates of `requests` on projects which depend on `plaid-python`, which seems like a problem.

In general, `setup.py` should be lenient and `requirements.txt` should be strict, as documented in Donald Stufft's [canonical article on the subject](https://caremad.io/posts/2013/07/setup-vs-requirement/).

Is there a specific reason that this requirement is pinned?

issue_title: Why wal-e wal-fetch/wal-prefetch is so slow?
issue_body:
Hi,

I don't know, weatherer somebody already raised this question or not, but here is my use-case:

We are running a lot of postgres clusters on AWS and using wal-e for continuous archiving.
Every replica is streaming from the master. Also, to be on the safe side if replication will break for some reason we have a `restore_command = ... wal-e wal-fetch ...`

When postgres is streaming everything is fine, but for example restart of replica takes enormous amount of time and CPU load is 100% until it will not start streaming again. It happens only because of `wal-e wal-fetch` is very slow :(

Luckily we don't need to restart very often, but...
We also have two replicas of AWS without streaming replication. The only this replicas can do - replay wal segments from S3. These replicas are running on m4.xlarge

m4.xlarge has 4 cpu cores, and we run `wal-e wal-fetch -p 2`, to limit amount of parallel prefetch jobs. CPU Utilization usually is 60%-90%.

I spend some time investigating why wal-e is so slow and it looks like the main problem is in `python startup cost` rather than downloading and extracting lzo file.
Also prefetch is implemented a little strange. What is the point to do a fork + exec wal-e wal-prefetch? Every exec call add high startup cost and increase CPU Utilization.

To prove my theory I did experiment and implemented nearly 100% functionality of `wal-e wal-fetch`
 in bash: https://github.com/zalando/spilo/blob/feature/wal-e-wal-fetch-bash/postgres-appliance/wal-e-wal-fetch.sh

It's very simple to use this script. Just run it instead of wal-e:
`restore_command = 'envdir "/path/to/wal-e/envdir" /usr/local/bin/wal-e-wal-fetch.sh wal-fetch -p 2 "%f" "%p"'`

Functionality is also pretty much the same: it can download files, it does prefetches and can promoted already prefetched files. Also it cleans up some stale files after itself...

And here are the results:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3407345/27764617-bb18d2ba-5e9d-11e7-8160-d416810c3df0.png)

Blue line on the graph shows CPU Utilization drop when I switched from wal-e to wal-e-wal-fetch.sh

maybe use

will be better, or mxnet should use a high performance timer rather than time.time
for example, using `time.perf_counter_ns` may be better

issue_title: Workers consuming from non-default celery queue receive tasks from default queue
issue_body:
We've setup our Airflow environment so that certain DAGs get sent to celery workers on specific machines.  I understand this is a bit of a unique situation.  We're using CeleryExecutors, and Celery is using RabbitMQ for the broker and Redis for the result backend.  

To get tasks routed to specific workers, I did the following:

- Setup a celery worker on machine X.  Within airflow.cfg on worker X, set the default_queue variable to be the name of the special queue, 'foobar'. Then, I started the celery worker on machine X like,
```
 airflow worker -q foobar
```
- To get DAGs to run on a specific worker, I modified the default_args to specify the queue to be 'foobar',
```
    default_args = {
    'owner': '...',
    'depends_on_past': False,
    'start_date': datetime(2016, 1, 26, 19, 0, 0),
    'email': ['casey@cogolabs.com'],
    'email_on_failure': True,
    'email_on_retry': False,
    'retries': 0,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'queue': 'foobar'}
```

At this point, my worker on machine X was consuming tasks from the foobar queue.  However, the worker was also consuming tasks from the default queue, but I hadn't specified the default queue when I started the celery worker on machine X.

It turns out that celery worker on machine X was listening to the Exchange used for the default queue (which I believe was named "celery") in addition to the foobar Exchange.  Even after manually removing that worker from the Exchange on the RabbitUI, the worker would be added back each time the worker was started.  To stop this, I modified celery_executor.py and added a line specifying the CELERY_DEFAULT_EXCHANGE,
```
class CeleryConfig(object):
    CELERY_ACCEPT_CONTENT = ['json', 'pickle']
    CELERYD_PREFETCH_MULTIPLIER = 1
    CELERY_ACKS_LATE = True
    BROKER_URL = configuration.get('celery', 'BROKER_URL')
    CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND = configuration.get('celery', 'CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND')
    CELERYD_CONCURRENCY = configuration.getint('celery', 'CELERYD_CONCURRENCY')
    CELERY_DEFAULT_QUEUE = DEFAULT_QUEUE
    CELERY_DEFAULT_EXCHANGE = DEFAULT_QUEUE # CHANGE 
```

After this change, the celery worker only connects to the Exchange associated with the queue it's listening to.  I also tested if the worker on X could still listen to both the default and foobar queues and that behavior seemed to be intact.
```
airflow worker -q foobar,default
```

So at this point, I'm curious what is the desired behavior?  To have workers that are listening to non-default queues only execute work for that specified queue?  Or have the worker execute work from both the specified non-default queue and the default queue?  I am by no means an expert on Celery or Rabbit so please share if I'm going about this the wrong way.

It's also worth noting that when I'd like to stop a worker from consuming from a queue I need to manually remove that worker from the Exchange (I've been doing that from the Rabbit UI http://rabbit_server:15672/#/exchanges) associated with that queue in addition to removing the name of the queue from the `airflow worker` call.

issue_title: Office Automation in dotnet core - are there any plans for this?
issue_body: Really the title says it all.  We want to build a conversion engine in .NET core that leverages the Office Automation components.  Nothing converts Office documents as well as Office Automation but we need to use dotnet core as our framework.  It is an edict where I work that the backend framework must be Open Source.

# Issue Title

It's impossible to target some framework version (in my case portable or sl5) without adding a full framework too.

# General

In one project I multi-target for a wide variety of frameworks including Silverlight 5, .NET 3.5 and portable. It worked fine in .NET 1.1 SDK. Here's an example project file.

``` xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <RootNamespace>Test</RootNamespace>
    <AssemblyName>Test</AssemblyName>
    <TargetFrameworks>sl5;netcoreapp1.1;net462;portable-net40+sl50+win8+wpa81+wp8</TargetFrameworks>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(TargetFramework)' == 'sl5'">
    <TargetFrameworkIdentifier>.NETFramework</TargetFrameworkIdentifier>
    <TargetFrameworkVersion>v4.5</TargetFrameworkVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(TargetFramework)' == 'portable-net40+sl50+win8+wpa81+wp8'">
    <TargetFrameworkIdentifier>.NETFramework</TargetFrameworkIdentifier>
    <TargetFrameworkVersion>v4.0</TargetFrameworkVersion>
  </PropertyGroup>

</Project>
```

Not sure why I need the PropertyGroups with conditions but it worked.

After updating to .NET Core 2.0 I'm seeing these errors:

```
Microsoft.PackageDependencyResolution.targets(165,5): error : Assets file 'obj\project.assets.json' doesn't have a target for '.NETFramework,Version=v4.5'. Ensure that restore has run and that you have included 'net45' in the TargetFrameworks for your project. [Test.csproj]
Microsoft.PackageDependencyResolution.targets(165,5): error : Assets file 'obj/project.assets.json' doesn't have a target for '.NETFramework,Version=v4.0'. Ensure that restore has run and that you have included 'net40' in the TargetFrameworks for your project. [Test.csproj]
```

The error isn't incorrect, adding `net40;net45` to `TargetFrameworks` does help. But it shouldn't be necessary and I don't really want to target them explicitly. Especially the .NET 4.0 seems redundant given that the portable profile I use does include that.

# Release builds are missing or pointing to a 404 page in Git Hub

All the links in this screen shot point to 404 pages. Why!?!?!?! People who depend on these particular versions (like me) can't find the binaries because they are no longer available in git hub or the asp.net core page.

issue_title: Any plans for fully implementing Future Payments in PayPal-Python-SDK?
issue_body:
I'm sorry if this is the improper place to ask. Please direct me to the proper channels if I'm asking in the wrong place, but my question regards PayPal-Python-SDK specifically, not PayPal as a whole, so I figure this is the best place to start.

We're interested in using Future Payments, but the documentation seems to assume mobile development for that feature. We're not, however, developing a mobile app and have no reason to create one.

Specifically, in order to do Future Payments, an authorization code using OAuth2 is required. There is nothing mobile-specific about OAuth2 so I wonder why it's mobile-specific at PayPal. Information on the matter seems to be scarce, and it is unclear whether this is a temporary arrangement or a design choice.

So what I'm wondering, ultimately is this: Is full support for Future Payments planned for PayPal-Python-SDK in the near future?

https://developer.paypal.com/docs/integration/mobile/make-future-payment/#get-an-auth-code

issue_title: Using `=` instead of `~` in a formula
issue_body:
I was wondering if using `~` instead of `=` in a formula string is to be compatible with the R formula syntax, or because using the latter will break something. If it won't break anything, could we optionally use `=` in the place of `~`? 

I know it is not a big deal, but I think a model statement with `=` would look more natural.

issue_title: Morningstar queries to some tickers fail because the API returns no volumes
issue_body:
(I am a newbie at this, please guide me on how to contribute effectively)

Running
pandas_datareader.data.DataReader('HSI','morningstar',start=datetime.date(2012,12,22),end=datetime.date(2012,12,22))
fails because the Morningstar query for HSI does not return 'volumes' information.

This is the response directly from the API:
{"DividendData":[],"FairValueList":null,"Max":49000,"Min":9800,"PriceDataList":[{"DailyMaxNumPoint":-1,"Datapoints":[[29578.01,NaN,NaN,NaN]],"DateIndexs":[43089],"Description":"","Max":29578.01,"Min":29578.01,"Slot":3,"StartPos":29578.01,"Symbol":"XHKG:HSI","chartType":0}],"PriceType":"",**"VolumeList":null**}

I do not know how many tickers behave like this.

This specific case can be fixed by modifying pandas_datareader/mstar/daily.py as follows:
-        volumes = jsondata["VolumeList"]["Datapoints"]
+        try:
+            volumes = jsondata["VolumeList"]["Datapoints"]
+        except:
+            volumes = len(dateidx) * [ float('nan') ]

            if self.incl_vol is True:
-                bardict.update({"Volume": int(volumes[p] * 1000000)})
+                try:
+                    bardict.update({"Volume": int(volumes[p] * 1000000)})
+                except:
+                    bardict.update({"Volume": volumes[p]})
            else:
                pass

There are other approaches - I do not know which one would be more aligned with the spirit of the module. Also, I have not investigated whether this change is safe in 100% of the use cases - it seems to be working fine for my project though.

Let me know if there is anything I can do to help!

issue_title: Revisiting global variable issues and ask for advice dealing with large amount of global data
issue_body:
This ticket will revisit #840 and #860. In #840 I was complaining about global section got loaded multiple times; the solution is to make them `shared`. This led to #860 a situation where `shared` does not work, though there is a workaround and we closed that ticket. However for situations like this:

```
[global]
data = yaml.load(...)

[default]
depends: data['some_file']
```

the `shared` option will not work. So `data` has to be global variable. The problem is, what if `yaml.load` results in a constant but has huge amount of data and gets loaded at every actual workflow steps? This is a waste of resource, as already been a problem in my applications. I do not know a way to get around it ...

issue_title: getting assertions from the zeromq lib
issue_body:
Hi
I am getting the following assertion
Assertion failed: nbytes == sizeof (dummy) (bundled\zermq\src\signaler.cpp:303)

i understand it comes from the zeromq library, but i can't understand how is it possible to get assertions on a release version, as i know assert command only works on debug compiled library

i wish to handle this assertions:
1. Are you using debug complied library
2. is there anyway to handle those assertions so they won't crush my process?

thanks

`libzmq`: 4.2.0
`pyzmq`: 16.06
`python`: 3.5.2
`system`: ubuntu 14.04 LTS Linux 3.16.7-ckt11-061515+ x86_64

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import sys
import zmq


def main (me, target=None):
    """
    Ubuntu 14.04 LTS, with misc. package updates.

    $ apt show libzmq | grep Version
    Version: 4.2.0-1
    $ uname -spr
    Linux 3.16.7-ckt11-061515+ x86_64

    Run in two terms like:
    term1$ python3 ./zmqtest.py T1
    term2$ python3 ./zmqtest.py T2 T1
    """

    print ('Python version:', sys.version)          # 3.5.2
    print ('libzmq version:', zmq.zmq_version())    # 4.2.0
    print ('PyZMQ version :', zmq.pyzmq_version())  # 16.0.2

    ctx = zmq.Context()
    socket = ctx.socket (zmq.ROUTER)

    if not target:
        socket.setsockopt (zmq.IDENTITY, me.encode ('utf-8'))
        socket.bind ('tcp://*:2060')

        print ('waiting for messages...')
        while True:
            socket.poll()
            print (socket.recv_multipart())

    else:
        def connect():
            nonlocal socket
            socket = ctx.socket (zmq.ROUTER)
            socket.setsockopt (zmq.IDENTITY, me.encode ('utf-8'))
            socket.connect ('tcp://localhost:2060')

        target = target.encode ('utf-8')
        connect()

        # This one is lost.
        socket.send_multipart ([target, b'HELLO #1'])
        for i in range (0, 100):
            # And all of these.
            socket.send_multipart ([target, b'HELLO #2'])

        # Makes no difference.
        # socket.close (linger=0)
        # connect()

        # Using copy=False, it seems to always make it.
        socket.send_multipart ([target, b'HELLO #3'], copy=False)

    socket.close (linger=0)
    ctx.term()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main (*sys.argv[1:])
```

issue_title: What is a best practice of using balanced consumer ?
issue_body:
Hello
I have a topic with 360-400 mio messages per day (~4000 msg/s) on Kafka 0.8.2 with 40 paritions.
So i am running 40 processes on 2 servers (20 per each)
Simple code is something like this:
pykafka v 1.0.3
```python
client = KafkaClient(hosts="server)
topic = client.topics['requests']
consumer = topic.get_balanced_consumer(consumer_group='Testing',auto_commit_enable=True,zookeeper_connect='amsmgmt002:2181,nas5:2181,amsdblx006:2181')

    process_message(message.value)

issue_title: --force-on-exists does not update notification_template when recipients change
issue_body:
## Description
In many cases where I run a;
```
>> tower-cli notification_template create --force-on-exists
```
The command runs an `no-op` when it should be updating the fields in the notification_template. If I create a test notification we can see the following are some examples;

## Example
1. Create a test notification.
```
[root@tower ~]# tower-cli notification_template create --name "test-notify" --description "Test Notify" --organization "Default" --notification-type email --status error --job-template 57 --sender tower@test.com --host localhost --recipients test@test.com --recipients test@test.com --port 25 --username '' --password '' --force-on-exists
Resource changed.
== =========== =================
id    name     notification_type
== =========== =================
 8 test-notify email
== =========== =================
```
2. Change nothing and get a `no-op` as expected.
```
[root@tower ~]# tower-cli notification_template create --name "test-notify" --description "Test Notify" --organization "Default" --notification-type email --status error --job-template 57 --sender tower@test.com --host localhost --recipients test@test.com --recipients test@test.com --port 25 --username '' --password '' --force-on-exists
OK. (changed: false)
```
3. Update `--recipients` email and get a `no-op`. Not Expected. Additionally it should probably be `--recipient` since you can only supply one email per flag.
```
[root@tower ~]# tower-cli notification_template create --name "test-notify" --description "Test Notify" --organization "Default" --notification-type email --status error --job-template 57 --sender tower@test.com --host localhost --recipients test@test.com --recipients different@test.com --port 25 --username '' --password '' --force-on-exists
OK. (changed: false)
```
4. Update `--port` and get `no-op`. Not Expected.
```
[root@tower ~]# tower-cli notification_template create --name "test-notify" --description "Test Notify" --organization "Default" --notification-type email --status error --job-template 57 --sender tower@test.com --host localhost --recipients test@test.com --recipients different@test.com --port 225 --username '' --password '' --force-on-exists
OK. (changed: false)
```
5. Update `--sender` and get `no-op`. Not Expected.
```
[root@tower ~]# tower-cli notification_template create --name "test-notify" --description "Test Notify" --organization "Default" --notification-type email --status error --job-template 57 --sender other@test.com --host localhost --recipients test@test.com --recipients different@test.com --port 225 --username '' --password '' --force-on-exists
OK. (changed: false)
```
6. Update SMTP `--host` and get `no-op`. Not Expected.
```
[root@tower ~]# tower-cli notification_template create --name "test-notify" --description "Test Notify" --organization "Default" --notification-type email --status error --job-template 57 --sender other@test.com --host different.com --recipients test@test.com --recipients different@test.com --port 225 --username '' --password '' --force-on-exists
OK. (changed: false)
```
7. Update `--job-template` and the notification is updated! 
```
[root@tower ~]# tower-cli notification_template create --name "test-notify" --description "Test Notify" --organization "Default" --notification-type email --status error --job-template 58 --sender other@test.com --host different.com --recipients test@test.com --recipients different@test.com --port 225 --username '' --password '' --force-on-exists
OK. (changed: true)
```
## Found In
```
[root@tower ~]# tower-cli --version
Tower CLI 3.2.1

[root@tower ~]# rpm -qa | grep tower
ansible-tower-ui-3.2.1-1.el7.x86_64
ansible-tower-venv-tower-3.2.1-1.el7.x86_64
ansible-tower-3.2.1-1.el7.x86_64
ansible-tower-server-3.2.1-1.el7.x86_64
ansible-tower-venv-ansible-3.2.1-1.el7.x86_64

issue_title: README / Docs should have guidelines for renew cronjob
issue_body:
I just installed `certbot` on a Debian box running my Wordpress blog. I have not yet moved to https because I want to ensure that my cert will be auto-renewed seamlessly without downtime. I looked over the docs and see that I can use `certbot renew` to renew my certificates and have a cronjob to take care of it.

I don't know what the interval should be. Since the certificate is *not renewed* if its still valid, my job could run hours before expiration and not be renewed and then run 24 hours later, causing a downtime of say 20 hours (if it last ran 4 hours before expiration and I run it every 24 hours).

How often should the cronjob be run? Running it every hour seems redundant given 6 month validity. Also, since `certbot` knows the expiration timestamp, can a deamon (say `certbotd`) run in the background and take care of renewal upon expiration without setting up a cronjob?

The thing is that cert paths are usually copied to another place (web server configs etc).

Now the successful output looks like this:
```
 - Congratulations! Your certificate and chain have been saved at
   /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com. Your cert
   will expire on 2666-06-06. To obtain a new or tweaked version of
   this certificate in the future, simply run certbot-auto again. To
   non-interactively renew *all* of your certificates, run
   "certbot-auto renew"
```

Also notice the dot after the cert path which is being selected after the double-click on the terminal:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/980679/27731421-8a80cc1a-5d95-11e7-9761-b0406e74a439.png)

I would also include key path there, it is needed in most usecases (`ssl_certificate_key` in nginx).

I think the following format would be way more usable:
```
 - Congratulations! Your certificate and chain have been saved at:
   /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com/fullchain.pem
   Key file has been saved at:
   /etc/letsencrypt/live/domain.com/privkey.pem
   Your cert will expire on 2666-06-06. To obtain a new or tweaked
   version of this certificate in the future, simply run
   certbot-auto again. To non-interactively renew *all* of your
   certificates, run "certbot-auto renew"
```

What do you think?

Now am not sure whether to revoke it and buy one like old days or do I wait for something to get fixed? Clearly **Let's Encrypt Authority X3** is not added as a trusted authority in certain cases. Please help. ![13234863_10209508144605528_1741411533_o](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2898596/15279498/955e27b8-1b42-11e6-8c36-e47ac7422a68.png) ![13241572_10156869238730392_1163547241_o](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2898596/15279499/955ec376-1b42-11e6-9686-ada4a2ec3732.jpg)

https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/5.3/indices-shrink-index.html

Depends on #656 which will bring the `change_settings` action (or something like that name)

The action file for a shrink will be a bit lengthy and complicated.  Here's an example of what it _may_ wind up looking like, **if the actions required were all broken out as separate actions**:

```
---
actions:
  1:
    action: allocation
    description: >- 
      Route all shards of indices onto the named node in preparation
      for shrinkage
    options:
      key: _name
      value: target_node_name
      allocation_type: require
      wait_for_completion: True
    filters:
    - filtertype: ...
  2:
    action: change_settings
    description: >-
      Change index settings to read-only for the selected indices
    options:
      settings:
        index.block.write: True
    filters:
    - filtertype: ...
  3:
    action: shrink
    description: >-
      Shrink the selected indices
    options:
      index: NEW_INDEX_NAME
      extra_settings:
        settings:
          index.number_of_replicas: 1
          index.number_of_shards: 1
          index.codec: best_compression 
        aliases:
          my_alias:
    filters:
    - filtertype: ...
  4: 
    action: change_settings   
      Change index settings to read-write for the selected indices
    options:
      settings:
        index.block.write: False
    filters:
    - filtertype: ...
  5:
    action: allocation
    description: >- 
      Remove or change routing for shards of indices so they migrate off the shrink node
    options:
      key: _name
      value: 
      allocation_type: require
      wait_for_completion: True
    filters:
    - filtertype: ...
```

It makes sense for this to all be encapsulated in a single shrink action, so that shard routing multiple indices doesn't all happen at once to a single node ( 💣 ).  In the end, the shrink action might look like this:

```
---
actions:
  1:
    action: shrink
    description: >-
      Shrink the selected indices on node FOO. 
      Re-route them (after shrinking) with the key/value/allocation_type settings.
    options:
      index: NEW_INDEX_NAME
      wait_for_completion: True
      shrink_node: FOO
      key: _name
      value: target_node_name
      allocation_type: require
      extra_settings:
        settings:
          index.number_of_replicas: 1
          index.number_of_shards: 1
          index.codec: best_compression 
        aliases:
          my_alias: 
    filters:
    - filtertype: ...
```
Of course, testing and the like will have to be done to hopefully see how much disk space is on FOO, and that `number_of_shards` is an acceptable factor for the provided indices, etc.

issue_title: Is batch size at all related to model geometry?
issue_body:
I've been running TED on 1080 Ti for a while now (it's around 18 hours in) and am now concerned that with the default batch sizes (16, 8, 8) I _may_ run into OOM issues.

Please could someone confirm if stopping a run and changing the batch size has any impact on the model geometry (_sorry, it's probably obvious to others, but I couldn't infer it from docs or looking over  issues_).

The reason I ask is that there is this point in [README.md](/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/README.md): 
> Be aware however that checkpoints are only valid for the same model geometry they had been generated from. In other words: If there are error messages of certain Tensors having incompatible dimensions, this is most likely due to an incompatible model change. One usual way out would be to wipe all checkpoint files in the checkpoint directory or changing it before starting the training.

And if changing the batch size changes the geometry then I  won't be able to continue later with the checkpoint from now.  I may well be out of options, but would rather avoid wasting that ~18 hrs if at all possible!!

Thank you
Neil

I generated 5 randn arrays and feed them into the pre-trained DenseNet in different ways. Predictions should be the same no matter the order I feed them in, whereas some things seem pretty wrong here. Can anyone help me with this?

The code is a bit long, but most are just repeating things:
`import torch`
`from torch.autograd import Variable`
`from torch import Tensor`
`from torchvision.models import densenet161`
`import numpy as np`
`densenet_full = densenet161(pretrained = True)`
`torch.manual_seed(2122)`

`sample_1 = torch.Tensor(torch.randn(3, 224, 224)).unsqueeze(0)`
`sample_2 = torch.Tensor(torch.randn(3, 224, 224)).unsqueeze(0)`
`sample_3 = torch.Tensor(torch.randn(3, 224, 224)).unsqueeze(0)`
`sample_4 = torch.Tensor(torch.randn(3, 224, 224)).unsqueeze(0)`
`variable_0 = Variable(sample_0)`
`variable_1 = Variable(sample_1)`
`variable_2 = Variable(sample_2)`
`variable_3 = Variable(sample_3)`
`variable_4 = Variable(sample_4)`
`variable_duo = Variable(torch.cat((sample_0, sample_1), dim=0))`
`variable_trio = Variable(torch.cat((sample_0, sample_1, sample_2), dim=0))`
`variable_quartet = Variable(torch.cat((sample_0, sample_1, sample_2, sample_3), dim=0))`
`variable_quintet = Variable(torch.cat((sample_0, sample_1, sample_2, sample_3, sample_4), dim=0))`

`pred_0 = densenet_full.forward(variable_0).data.numpy()`
`pred_1 = densenet_full.forward(variable_1).data.numpy()`
`pred_2 = densenet_full.forward(variable_2).data.numpy()`
`pred_3 = densenet_full.forward(variable_3).data.numpy()`
`pred_4 = densenet_full.forward(variable_4).data.numpy()`

`pred_duo = densenet_full.forward(variable_duo).data.numpy()`
`pred_trio = densenet_full.forward(variable_trio).data.numpy()`
`pred_quartet = densenet_full.forward(variable_quartet).data.numpy()`
`pred_quintet = densenet_full.forward(variable_quintet).data.numpy()`

`print('Prediction for the 0th random number is: {}'.format(np.argmax(pred_0)))`
`print('Prediction for the 1st random number is: {}'.format(np.argmax(pred_1)))`
`print('Prediction for the 2nd random number is: {}'.format(np.argmax(pred_2)))`
`print('Prediction for the 3rd random number is: {}'.format(np.argmax(pred_3)))`
`print('Prediction for the 4th random number is: {}'.format(np.argmax(pred_4)))`

`print('Predictions for the two random numbers are: {} and {} respectively.'
       .format(np.argmax(pred_duo, axis=1)[0], 
              np.argmax(pred_duo, axis=1)[1]))`
`print('Predictions for the three random numbers are: {}, {} and {} respectively.'
      .format(np.argmax(pred_trio, axis=1)[0], 
              np.argmax(pred_trio, axis=1)[1], 
              np.argmax(pred_trio, axis=1)[2]))`
`print('Predictions for the four random numbers are: {}, {}, {} and {} respectively.'
      .format(np.argmax(pred_quartet, axis=1)[0], 
              np.argmax(pred_quartet, axis=1)[1], 
              np.argmax(pred_quartet, axis=1)[2], 
              np.argmax(pred_quartet, axis=1)[3]))`
`print('Predictions for the five random numbers are: {}, {}, {}, {} and {} respectively.'
      .format(np.argmax(pred_quintet, axis=1)[0], 
              np.argmax(pred_quintet, axis=1)[1], 
              np.argmax(pred_quintet, axis=1)[2], 
              np.argmax(pred_quintet, axis=1)[3],
              np.argmax(pred_quintet, axis=1)[4]))`

Hi Dustin,

What if at every split we just sample two items and compute the hyperplane that separates them?

For angular distance you would normalize the two items first. This would also generalize to arbitrary pseudometrics nicely: Manhattan, KL-divergence, etc.

For highdimensional distributions where there's structure in the data, this is more likely to sample from that structure. It will also be much faster in general since we are guaranteed to split the point set in two subsets

Is there a way I can see (for example) a list after running the pipeline with the info about the completion times? That would be really useful. I can see the time when the task actually runs but after it's finished I can't see it anywhere.

I'm trying to create a convolutional autoencoder (which, when stable, will hopefully be pegged for submission to /examples/).

However, I'm always getting NaN between my highest conv_2d layer and lowest conv_2d_transpose layer. Sometimes it's gradients, though the flavor of the last dozen crashes or so is the good ole ReLU. I've tried quite a few things (learning rate [currently .001], different bias/weight init patterns, a variety of activation functions, differing layer depths, batch sizes 1-8, etc.). However, it doesn't seem to even make it to 100 iterations. The data's just imagenet people, and my loss is reasonable (between .02 and .006--the loss for the particular problem I'm working on is lower than standard.)

Maybe it's poor network design, but I'm not fully convinced yet that that's the case. Regardless of my configuration, the lowest conv_2d_transpose layer always is the one that triggers the NaN. Once or twice it was the second lowest, but that was when I cranked up the learning rates very high to watch the gradients for distinct patterns. I've had to wrangle the conv_2d_transpose layer a bit to get it to cooperate fully, and plan on trying to expand that functionality within tflearn once I get some free cycles. In brief, here's my net design:

network = input_data(shape=[None, 224, 224, 3])
network = conv_2d(network, 64, 3, activation='leaky_relu', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')
network = conv_2d(network, 64, 3, activation='leaky_relu', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')
network = conv_2d_transpose(network, 32, [3, 3, 32, 64], [1, 1, 1, 1], [112, 112, 32], activation='leaky_relu', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')
network = conv_2d_transpose(network, 16, [3, 3, 16, 32], [1, 2, 2, 1], [224, 224, 16], activation='leaky_relu', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')
network = conv_2d_transpose(network, 2, [3, 3, 2, 16], [1, 1, 1, 1], [224, 224, 2], activation='sigmoid', weights_init='truncated_normal', bias='truncated_normal')

issue_title: Add ability to empty a reference part property (no id selected)

issue_body:
This library [creates a new `Session` for each request](https://github.com/Mangopay/mangopay2-python-sdk/blob/v2/mangopaysdk/tools/resttool.py#L110), which means that each one opens a new HTTP(S) connection, and that's a lot of overhead when making more than a few requests.

Background and details: https://github.com/liberapay/liberapay.com/issues/382.

Pull Request coming soon.

issue_title: `arith_util::is_irrational_algebraic_numeral` fails to check sanity of arguments
issue_body:
Here is a simple demonstration of what could trigger this bug
```
(set-option :numeral-as-real true)
(declare-const x Real)

(simplify (acos (/ 2)))
```

I would fix this but I am not sure if the issue should be tackled at the `simplify_cmd` level or if the arithmetic utility functions should be changed to return an errno.

issue_title: Wrong results in optimization query using Java API

issue_body:
The `ASTRef` class contains two definitions of the `__deepcopy__` function.

The first one, which is incidentally the oldest _and_ the correct one, is at [z3.py#L286](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/api/python/z3/z3.py#L286)

The second one, which does not conform to the deepcopy spec, is at 
[z3.py#L371](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/src/api/python/z3/z3.py#L371) and was introduced by 8dadd30db5e1f09739bcb877e9dc5fb3240b1cf9 

The consequence is only the second (wrong) one is actually registered with the class, so errors like [this](https://github.com/MAIAN-tool/MAIAN/issues/6) can happen.

If it is any help, this is a class of problems pylint identifies and warns against so it could be useful to run it against your python API.

PR to follow.

When I try to run Z3 version 4.5.0 on some of my windows machines, I get an error that it is missing the file VCOMP110.DLL.
